title: Hispania
subtitle: 300–19 BC · Carthage, Rome and the Wars of Resistance

categories.label: Power
categories.subtitle: Carthaginian expansion, Roman conquest and the indigenous resistance

entries.label: Hamilcar Barca founds Carthaginian Hispania
entries.hint: After the First Punic War defeat, Carthage compensates by conquering southern Iberia — Hamilcar Barca lands at Gadir (Cádiz) in 237 BC and systematically builds a Barcid empire; his son Hasdrubal founds Carthago Nova (modern Cartagena) c.227 BC as the capital of Carthaginian Hispania.
entries.note: Polybius II.1.5–8: primary source for Hamilcar's campaign (Polybius uses Iberian informants). The founding of Carthago Nova: Polybius II.13. Hasdrubal's treaty with Rome (226 BC) fixing the Ebro as boundary: Polybius II.13.7. The Barcid family's Iberian empire: documented through coins (Cartagena numismatic museum) and Polybius. Richardson (1986), Hispaniae: Spain and the Development of Roman Imperialism: the standard modern study.

entries.label: Scipio Africanus conquers Carthago Nova
entries.hint: Scipio Africanus — aged 25 and never having held a consulship — takes command in Hispania in 210 BC; in 209 BC he captures Carthago Nova in a single assault, taking the Carthaginian treasury and silver mines; the capture is the turning point of the Punic War in Iberia.
entries.note: Polybius X.9–15: detailed account of the Carthago Nova assault — primary source (Polybius had access to participants). Livy XXVI.41–51: parallel account. The silver mines: Polybius X.10 documents their output. The campaign's audacity: Scipio attacked on one side while the tide cut off the other — a tactical detail confirmed by both Polybius and archaeology. Liddell Hart (1926), A Greater Than Napoleon: Scipio Africanus: contextualises the achievement.

entries.label: Caesar and Augustus — Hispania in the Roman civil wars
entries.loc: Hispalis (Seville); Munda (battle 45 BC); Emerita Augusta (founded 25 BC)
entries.hint: Hispania is the arena for Julius Caesar's Spanish campaigns (49, 45 BC) — the Battle of Munda (45 BC) is his last great battle; Augustus founds Emerita Augusta (Mérida) in 25 BC for veterans; his reorganisation of Hispania into three provinces is the administrative foundation for four centuries of Roman governance.
entries.note: Caesar, Civil War III: primary source for his Spanish campaigns. The Battle of Munda 17 Mar 45 BC: Appian, Civil Wars II.103–105. Emerita Augusta founded 25 BC: Cassius Dio LIII.26 (primary). Augustus's provincial reorganisation: documented in the Res Gestae Divi Augusti (primary — the emperor's own account). The Lex Irnitana (bronze tablet, 1st c. AD, Museo Arqueológico Nacional): documents the detailed administration of a Hispanian municipium.

categories.label: Conflict
categories.subtitle: Punic Wars, the Lusitanian rebellion and the siege of Numantia

entries.label: Siege of Saguntum — the spark of the Second Punic War
entries.hint: Hannibal besieges Saguntum for eight months — when the city falls Hannibal plunders it and the survivors are killed or enslaved; later Roman sources (Livy) add a dramatic mass self-immolation that Polybius, the more contemporary source, does not record; the gap between the two accounts is itself a primary source on how Rome mythologised the war's origins.
entries.note: Polybius III.17 (primary, most contemporary): describes the capture and plunder but no mass suicide. Livy XXI.14: the self-immolation tradition — written c.200 years later. The discrepancy: scholarly consensus follows Polybius; Livy's account reflects Roman moralising tradition. The Saguntine coins (Arse series): numismatic primary evidence. The Roman theatre of Saguntum (1st c. AD): documents full romanisation after the war.

entries.label: Battle of Ilipa — the final Carthaginian defeat in Iberia
entries.loc: Ilipa (near modern Alcalá del Río, Seville)
entries.hint: Scipio defeats a numerically superior Carthaginian army at Ilipa through a tactical manoeuvre that reverses his battle order at the last moment — the most tactically sophisticated battle of the Punic Wars in Iberia; after Ilipa, Carthage's Iberian empire is finished.
entries.note: Polybius XI.20–24 (primary; detailed tactical account). Livy XXVIII.12–16 (parallel). The battle's tactical innovation: Scipio reverses his usual order of battle — strong wings, weak centre — documented precisely in Polybius. The Carthaginian numbers (70,000 foot, 4,000 horse, 32 elephants): Polybius XI.20.2. Lazenby (1978), Hannibal's War: the standard modern tactical analysis.

entries.label: Viriathus — the Lusitanian who humiliated Rome
entries.hint: Viriathus, a Lusitanian shepherd turned general, defeats Roman armies eight times in eight years — forcing Quintus Fabius Maximus Servilianus to sign a humiliating peace treaty (141/140 BC); his adoptive brother Quintus Servilius Caepio then convinces the Senate to repudiate the treaty and bribes Viriathus's own envoys to murder him (139 BC).
entries.note: Appian, Iberian Wars 60–75: primary source. The peace treaty with Servilianus: Appian IB.65–69 — the Senate refused to ratify. Caepio's role: Appian IB.69 — he succeeded Servilianus, despised the treaty, lobbied the Senate to annul it, and bribed the assassins. Viriathus's assassination: Appian IB.74 — 'Rome does not pay traitors.' Richardson (1986), Hispaniae ch.6: the standard modern account.

entries.label: Siege of Numantia — the city that chose death over surrender
entries.hint: Scipio Aemilianus builds a 9km circumvallation wall around Numantia with seven camps — after 13 months the surviving Numantines burn their city and many kill themselves rather than be enslaved; the symbolic end of the Celtiberian Wars, though organised resistance continues in subsequent decades.
entries.note: Appian, Iberian Wars 76–98: primary source — draws on Polybius who was present as Scipio's friend. The circumvallation: 9km wall confirmed archaeologically. Excavations at Renieblas (Scipio's camps): ongoing since 2015 (Cambridge Scholars Publishing 2018 publication). Carbon-14 of Numantia city wall base: dated 340±50 BCE — confirms pre-Roman occupation. The Numantine ceramics at block XXIII: primary archaeological evidence for the 133 BC destruction layer.

entries.label: Sertorian War — Rome's rebel province
entries.hint: Quintus Sertorius, a Roman general of the Marian faction, creates a quasi-independent state in Hispania with strong indigenous support — he establishes a shadow Senate, founds a school at Osca for the sons of Iberian chiefs, and holds off Pompey and Metellus for eight years; assassinated at a banquet in 72 BC.
entries.note: Plutarch, Life of Sertorius: primary biography — documents the school at Osca (Sert.14) and the shadow Senate (Sert.22). Appian, Civil Wars I.86–115. The school at Osca: 'he gathered around him the sons of the most distinguished Iberians and gave them a Roman education' — primary source for Roman-Iberian cultural integration under duress. Konrad (1994), Plutarch's Sertorius: the standard modern study. The Sertorian War is the largest gap in the 133–27 BC narrative and the clearest example of Iberian participation in Roman politics.

entries.label: Cantabrian Wars — Rome's last Iberian conquest
entries.hint: The Cantabrian and Asturian tribes of the northern mountains are the last independent holdouts in Iberia — Augustus himself commands the early campaigns (26–25 BC) and is invalided out; Marcus Agrippa finally breaks resistance in 19 BC; the Cantabrian Wars are the bloodiest phase of Roman conquest in the peninsula and the true end of indigenous resistance.
entries.note: Cassius Dio LIII.25–26 (primary for Augustus's campaigns). Florus II.33: account of the wars. Augustus's personal command: documented — he falls ill and withdraws. Agrippa's final campaign 19 BC: documented in Cassius Dio LIV.5. The foundation of Emerita Augusta (25 BC) and Caesaraugusta (14 BC): direct results of the Cantabrian campaigns — documented. Syme (1934), The Spanish War of Augustus: the standard modern study. The Cantabrian Wars extend the folio's resistance narrative to its actual conclusion.

entries.label: Battle of Munda — Caesar's last battle
entries.hint: Julius Caesar defeats the sons of Pompey at Munda in his most desperately contested battle — he later says he had often fought for victory but at Munda he had fought for his life; the final military act of the Roman civil wars on Iberian soil.
entries.note: Caesar's remark: Suetonius, Divus Julius 36 — 'he had often fought for victory, but at Munda he had fought for his life.' Appian, Civil Wars II.103–105: detailed account. Bellum Hispaniense (by an unknown officer, primary): the most detailed account of the Spanish campaign. The battle site: disputed — Montilla or Osuna most commonly identified.

categories.label: Order
categories.subtitle: Roman provincial administration and the Lex Provinciae

entries.label: Division of Hispania into two provinces — 197 BC
entries.hint: Rome creates two permanent provincial praetors for Hispania in 197 BC — Hispania Citerior (Nearer Spain, with Tarraco as its main Roman base) and Hispania Ulterior (Further Spain, where early governors operated from Gadir and Hispalis); this is the first formal Roman provincial administration in Iberia. Corduba is not founded until c.169–152 BC.
entries.note: Livy XXXII.28: records the creation of two permanent praetors for Spain in 197 BC — primary source. Corduba's founding c.169–152 BC: Strabo III.2.1; the city postdates the 197 BC division. Tarraco as a Roman military base from 218 BC: confirmed archaeologically; its formal designation as provincial capital is Augustan. Richardson (1986), Hispaniae ch.3: the standard analysis of early provincial organisation. The later Lex Irnitana (c.91 AD, Museo Arqueológico de Sevilla) documents how this provincial framework functioned in practice — see Roman Hispania 27BC–409AD folio.

categories.label: Belief
categories.subtitle: Iberian religion, the cult of Hercules Gaditanus and Roman polytheism

entries.label: Sanctuary of Hercules Gaditanus — Phoenician-Roman cult
entries.date: c.1100 BC onwards (with Roman period peak)
entries.hint: The sanctuary of Melqart/Hercules at Gadir is one of the oldest continuously worshipped sites in the Western Mediterranean — Phoenician, Carthaginian and Roman in succession; Julius Caesar reportedly wept here at a statue of Alexander the Great; Polybius documents its pre-Roman prestige.
entries.note: Polybius III.33 (on the sanctuary's prestige). Caesar's weeping: Suetonius, Divus Julius 7 (primary). Strabo III.5.3: describes the sanctuary at length — primary geographical source. The sanctuary's Phoenician origins: Diodorus Siculus V.20. Koch (1984), Hispanien und Karthago: the standard study of Punic religious influence. The site today: partially documented through underwater archaeology off Cádiz.

categories.label: Expression
categories.subtitle: Iberian art, the Dama de Elche and Carthaginian material culture

entries.label: Dama de Elche — Iberian sculpture
entries.loc: Elche (La Alcudia excavation, 1897); now Museo Arqueológico Nacional, Madrid
entries.hint: The Dama de Elche is the masterpiece of Iberian sculpture — a stone bust of a woman with elaborate headdress, jewellery and holes that may have held a funerary urn; found 1897; acquired by the Louvre then returned to Spain 1941; the most important pre-Roman artistic object from Iberia.
entries.note: Discovery: 4 Aug 1897, La Alcudia excavation, Elche. Museo Arqueológico Nacional, Madrid (MAN). Date: c.4th century BC (contested). Chapa Brunet (2003), La escultura ibérica: the standard study. The hole in the back: suggests use as a funerary urn — confirmed by comparison with other Iberian bust-urns. The Louvre acquisition (1897) and repatriation (1941): documented in both French and Spanish museum records.

entries.label: Carthago Nova — the Barcid capital
entries.hint: Hasdrubal founds Carthago Nova c.227 BC as the capital of Barcid Hispania — controlling the richest silver mines in the Western Mediterranean; Scipio's conquest (209 BC) gives Rome access to these mines; the Roman theatre of Cartagena (1st c. BC) is built on the Carthaginian layers.
entries.note: Polybius X.10: documents the silver mines — 40,000 men working them, yielding 25,000 drachmas daily to Carthage (primary). The Roman theatre of Cartagena: extant (restored, opened as museum 2008) — overlies Carthaginian levels. The Carthaginian town plan: documented archaeologically. González Simancas (1905): early systematic excavation. The mine archaeology: Domergue (1990), Les mines de la péninsule ibérique: the standard study.

entries.label: Iberian bronze votive figurines — musicians documented
entries.loc: Various sites; Museo Arqueológico Nacional, Madrid
entries.hint: Bronze votive figurines from Iberian sanctuaries include musicians playing double flutes (aulos) — the best evidence for music in pre-Roman Iberia; they are dedicated at sanctuaries and document both the instruments and the ritual context of Iberian musical performance.
entries.note: Iberian bronze figurines: MAN Madrid and Museo de Jaén hold major collections. The musician figurines: catalogued in Lillo Carpio (1993), El santuario ibérico de La Luz (Murcia). Cerdeño & García Huerta (1992) on Iberian votive practices. The double flute (tibia) figurines: the most common musical representation. The sanctuaries as performance spaces: documented in excavation records from La Serreta (Alcoy) and other sites.

entries.label: Dama de Baza — Iberian sculpture in its funerary context
entries.loc: Baza (Granada) — excavation 1971; Museo Arqueológico Nacional, Madrid
entries.hint: The Dama de Baza was found in 1971 fully intact within its original chamber tomb at Baza, Granada — still bearing original polychrome paint, flanked by weapons and funerary offerings; unlike the Dama de Elche (isolated when discovered), this provides the complete archaeological and artistic context: a seated goddess or priestess figure used as a funerary urn.
entries.note: HARD ANCHOR: Dama de Baza, Tomb 155, Cerro del Santuario necropolis, Baza, July 1971: Museo Arqueológico Nacional (MAN), Madrid. Excavation director: Francisco Presedo Velo — full excavation report published 1982 (Ministerio de Cultura). The original polychrome pigments: documented by laboratory analysis. The hollow back for funerary ashes: confirmed. Chapa Brunet (2003), La escultura ibérica: the standard study. Unlike the Elche bust, the Baza sculpture's tomb context gives it unique evidential value for Iberian religious and gender practice.

entries.label: San Miguel de Liria pottery — Iberian narrative scenes
entries.loc: Edeta (mod. Llíria, Valencia); Museo de Prehistoria, Valencia
entries.hint: The painted pottery from San Miguel de Liria (ancient Edeta) is the primary visual source for indigenous Iberian life — its vessels carry detailed narrative scenes of armed combat, naval battles, ritual dances, processions and mythological figures; the only surviving figurative art documenting Iberian dress, weapons, choreography and ceremony in their own visual language.
entries.note: San Miguel de Liria excavations: Museo de Prehistoria de Valencia — primary collection. The narrative pottery corpus: Ballester et al. (1954), Cerámica del Cerro de San Miguel de Liria: the foundational study. Bonet Rosado (1995), El Tossal de Sant Miquel de Llíria: the standard modern excavation report. The Iberian script inscriptions on the same vessels: partially deciphered (HESPERIA database). The scenes of dance (including a woman's dance depicted in detail): the best evidence for pre-Roman Iberian performing arts.

categories.label: Thought
categories.subtitle: Greek and Roman geographic writing on Iberia

entries.label: Strabo's Geography — the primary literary source for Iberia
entries.hint: Strabo's Geography Books III–IV cover Iberia in detail — drawing on Polybius, Artemidorus, Posidonius and his own research; it is the most systematic ancient geographical account of the peninsula and documents physical geography, resources, peoples, cities and Roman administration.
entries.note: Strabo, Geography III (Iberia): Loeb Classical Library (Jones translation 1923). Strabo draws on Polybius (who visited Iberia) and Artemidorus (who surveyed the coastline c.100 BC). The description of the Guadalquivir valley, the silver mines, and the Celtiberian peoples: primary literary source. Roller (2018), A Historical and Topographical Guide to the Geography of Strabo: the standard modern commentary.

entries.label: Appian's Iberian Wars — the primary narrative of Roman conquest
entries.hint: Appian's Iberica (part of the Roman History) is the primary narrative source for the entire Roman conquest of Iberia — drawing on Polybius (eyewitness of the Numantine campaign), it preserves information not found elsewhere; the Numantine War section is the most detailed surviving account of any Iberian resistance.
entries.note: Appian, Iberica: Loeb edition (White translation). Appian's source for Numantia: explicitly Polybius, who was present — Princeton Encyclopedia notes 'Appian obtained his information from Polybios, a friend and chronicler of Scipio and an eyewitness of the siege'. Richardson (2000), Appian: Wars of the Romans in Iberia: the standard modern translation with commentary.

categories.label: Economy
categories.subtitle: Iberian silver, Carthaginian mines and Roman exploitation

entries.label: Sierra Morena silver mines — the wealth of Hispania
entries.loc: Sierra Morena; Carthago Nova (Cartagena) mining district
entries.hint: The silver mines of southern Hispania are the richest in the Western Mediterranean — Polybius (preserved in Strabo) documents 40,000 workers at the Carthago Nova mines under Roman administration producing 25,000 drachmas daily for the Roman treasury; the Carthaginians had exploited the same mines before Rome; the mines fund Rome's imperial expansion.
entries.note: Strabo III.2.10 (preserving Polybius): '40,000 workers, 25,000 drachmas daily for the Roman people' — the workers are under Roman, not Carthaginian, management at the time of writing. Polybius X.10 on the Roman capture of Carthago Nova's treasury (209 BC): separate passage. Domergue (1990), Les mines de la péninsule ibérique: the comprehensive modern study. The Carthaginian use of the same mines before 209 BC: inferred from their strategic importance but less directly documented than the Roman-era output figures.

entries.label: Olive oil and wine — Hispania as Rome's breadbasket
entries.hint: After Rome takes Hispania, the Guadalquivir valley (Baetica) becomes one of Rome's most productive agricultural provinces — Dressel 20 amphoras carrying Baetican olive oil are found across the entire Roman Empire; Monte Testaccio in Rome is a mountain of smashed olive-oil amphoras from Baetica.
entries.note: Dressel 20 amphoras: the most common amphora type found in Roman contexts across the empire — Museo Arqueológico Nacional holds examples. Monte Testaccio, Rome: estimated 53 million amphoras, predominantly from Baetica — documented by Rodríguez Almeida (1984). Mattingly (1988), Oil for Export? A Comparison of Libyan, Spanish and Tunisian Olive Oil Production: the standard study. The sheer volume of Baetican exports: the most concrete economic evidence for Hispania's productivity.

categories.label: Exchange
categories.subtitle: Phoenician trading networks, Carthaginian colonisation and Roman integration

entries.label: Phoenician-Punic trading network — Gadir and beyond
entries.hint: Gadir (Cádiz) is the oldest continuously inhabited city in Western Europe — founded by Phoenicians c.1100 BC as a trading post; the Phoenician-Punic commercial network from Gadir to Carthage to the Eastern Mediterranean is the pre-Roman globalisation of Iberia.
entries.note: The foundation of Gadir c.1100 BC: Velleius Paterculus I.2 (cites Timaeus); Strabo III.5.5. The Phoenician archaeological evidence: excavations under modern Cádiz (difficult due to urban overlay) — documented in Aubet (1993), The Phoenicians and the West: the standard study. The Phoenician cemetery at Villaricos and other sites: documented in Museo Arqueológico Nacional collections.

entries.label: Iberian script — a writing system still partially undeciphered
entries.hint: The Iberians develop their own script (adapting Phoenician/Greek) c.5th century BC — over 2,000 inscriptions survive; the script is partially deciphered (its phonetic values are known) but the language remains poorly understood; it is one of the major unsolved linguistic puzzles of antiquity.
entries.note: The Iberian script corpus: HESPERIA database (online, University of Valencia) — over 2,000 inscriptions. The script's partial decipherment: the letters' values are known from bilingual Iberian-Latin and Iberian-Greek inscriptions; the language's grammar and vocabulary remain largely opaque. Untermann (1975–1997), Monumenta Linguarum Hispanicarum (4 vols): the standard corpus. The linguistic isolation: Iberian has no known relatives.

categories.label: World
categories.subtitle: Iberia between Carthage and Rome — a contested periphery

entries.label: Ebro Treaty (226 BC) — Iberia as a Roman sphere
entries.hint: Rome and Carthage agree the Ebro as the boundary of their spheres of influence in Iberia — everything north of the Ebro belongs to Rome; Saguntum, south of the line but an ally of Rome, becomes the ambiguous case that triggers the Second Punic War.
entries.note: Polybius II.13.7: primary source for the Ebro treaty. The Saguntum complication: Polybius III.17 — the treaty's ambiguity is the legal cause of the war. The modern historiographic debate about the treaty's exact terms: Eckstein (1987), Senate and General, ch.4. The treaty as a document defining spheres of interest rather than formal sovereignty: the first such agreement in the Western Mediterranean.

categories.label: Lives
categories.subtitle: Gender, sexuality & marginalised experience · evidence anchored

entries.label: Slave labour in the Carthago Nova silver mines
entries.hint: Polybius documents 40,000 workers in the Carthago Nova silver mines — the largest single documented concentration of slave labour in the ancient Western Mediterranean; these are not free workers but enslaved people; their conditions are described as extreme in Diodorus Siculus.
entries.note: Polybius X.10.12: '40,000 workers, yielding 25,000 drachmas daily to Carthage' — the primary figure. Diodorus Siculus V.36–38: describes mine conditions — 'many die because of the extreme severity of their treatment'. Domergue (1990): documents the slaves' physical evidence (skeletal remains showing mine injuries). This is the best-documented large-scale slave labour operation in ancient Iberia.

entries.label: Devoting Numantia — mass suicide as documented resistance
entries.hint: When Numantia finally falls, the surviving 4,000 inhabitants burn their city and kill themselves — refusing to be enslaved; Appian describes individual acts of resistance including men killing their families before themselves; the destruction layer is archaeologically documented.
entries.note: Appian, Iberian Wars 97–98: the most detailed account of the city's end. The burning layer: documented in excavations at block XXIII (Numantia archaeology) — carbon-dated to 133 BC. The skeletal evidence: remains showing signs of starvation and violence documented in the excavations. The site today: Museo Numantino (Soria) and in situ remains at Garray. Appian's source: Polybius, who was present at the siege.

entries.label: Devotio — Iberian warriors who consecrated their lives
entries.loc: Iberia (attested across Celtiberian and Lusitanian zones)
entries.hint: The devotio is a uniquely Iberian institution — a warrior consecrates his life to a chieftain in a sacred oath; if the chieftain dies in battle the sworn devotees are religiously and socially obligated to die with him, as their lives belong to the deceased; Roman writers document it with a mixture of admiration and horror; Viriathus's bodyguards practice it.
entries.note: Plutarch, Life of Sertorius 14 (mentions the institution). Appian, Iberian Wars 70–74: Viriathus's devotees documented — after his assassination his followers mourn him 'as a god' and their obligation continues. Diodorus Siculus V.34: the fullest ancient description of the devotio practice. Brunaux (1988), The Celtic Gauls: comparative analysis. Quesada Sanz (1994), 'Voto, Devoción y Botín' in Iconografía Ibérica: the standard modern study of the institution in archaeological context. The devotio is the most distinctive feature of Iberian warrior psychology documented in multiple sources.

events.l: Battle of Munda — Caesar's last battle
Cantabrian Wars end — Iberia fully Roman
Dama de Baza — funerary sculpture in context
Baetican olive oil — Roman exports begin
